Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd is the claimant in Originating Claim No 475 of 2023.
AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd has three shareholders, namely Liew Nyok Wah, Teo Ying Ping, and Ho Siow Poh.
Ho Siow Poh held the position of Managing Director of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd and was its sole director on record.
AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd was incorporated on 5 August 2020 with a paid-up capital of $100,000.
Upon incorporation, Teo Ying Ping and Liew Nyok Wah each held 33% of the shares in AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, while Ho Siow Poh held 34%.

Shortly after the incorporation of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, Liew Shulin Mrs Alexander Chong, the daughter of Liew Nyok Wah, joined the company and was involved in its finance and business operations.
In 2021, Marcus Teo Xian Jun, the son of Teo Ying Ping, joined AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd and was subsequently appointed its Chief Financial Officer.
Throughout the life of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, Teo Ying Ping and Liew Nyok Wah contributed funds towards the company.
At the inception of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, the financial contributions by the shareholders were made from their personal funds.

Later in time, some of the financial contributions to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd were made by procuring loans from third parties to the shareholders, with onward transfer of funds to the company or directly to the company.
In or around May 2023, disagreements over the funding arrangements of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd caused the shareholders to fall out.
AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd commenced an action against Liew Nyok Wah on 25 July 2023.
On 27 July 2023, the employment of Liew Shulin Mrs Alexander Chong with AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd was terminated.
At trial, evidence on behalf of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd was given by Ho Siow Poh, Teo Ying Ping, and Marcus Teo Xian Jun.

AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd pleaded that there was an agreement between the three shareholders that Teo Ying Ping and Liew Nyok Wah would fund the company with matching dollar-to-dollar capital injections, referred to as the Agreement.
AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd pleaded that the Agreement to fund the company was entered into between the three shareholders and not with the company itself.
There was no written document evidencing the alleged Agreement for matching capital injections.
In his affidavit of evidence-in-chief, Ho Siow Poh stated that Teo Ying Ping and Liew Nyok Wah had agreed to inject $500,000 of capital each into AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd.

Under cross-examination, Ho Siow Poh admitted that while he had proposed a paid-up capital sum of $1,000,000, Teo Ying Ping and Liew Nyok Wah only agreed to provide an initial capital sum of $50,000 each.
In his affidavit of evidence-in-chief, Ho Siow Poh referred to Teo Ying Ping and Liew Nyok Wah being obliged to provide 'funds or capital injections' to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd.

Under cross-examination, Teo Ying Ping agreed that AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd was not concerned about the source of funding and was only concerned that it had funding to carry on business operations.

Teo Ying Ping testified that if AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd made profits, it should repay his and Liew Nyok Wah’s contributions first, and the balance would be divided between the shareholders according to their respective shareholdings.

In its closing submissions, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd adopted the position that if the company made profits, it should first repay the contributions of Teo Ying Ping and Liew Nyok Wah before distributing any balance according to shareholdings.
The financial statements of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd and its filings with the Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority showed that its share capital remained at $100,000.
Under cross-examination, when asked why the share capital of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd remained at $100,000 in the records, Ho Siow Poh stated that he was focused on running the company.
The three shareholders agreed that AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd would pay interest on the funds provided by Teo Ying Ping and Liew Nyok Wah.
Interest was paid on contributions made by the shareholders only after the initial injection of $100,000 for the paid-up capital of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd.
On 7 April 2021, a contribution of $100,000 associated with Teo Ying Ping was made to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd.

In a WhatsApp message to Liew Shulin, Teo Ying Ping stated that he had passed a cheque for $100,000 dated 1 April 2021 to Ho Siow Poh and that it was for a short term of two months with an interest rate of 1.5% per month.
Under cross-examination, Teo Ying Ping accepted that the $100,000 contribution made on 7 April 2021 was a short-term loan.
In Further and Better Particulars dated 27 September 2023, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd included the $100,000 contribution of 7 April 2021 in a table of 'capital' contributions.
The table of 'capital' contributions was reproduced in the affidavit of evidence-in-chief of Ho Siow Poh and was confirmed by Teo Ying Ping in his own affidavit of evidence-in-chief.
Of the $2.22 million that AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd claimed was provided by Teo Ying Ping as 'capital', at least $300,000 was a loan taken by the company from Junk Yard Dog Ventures Pte Ltd.
Under cross-examination, Teo Ying Ping conceded that there was no question that the $300,000 from Junk Yard Dog Ventures Pte Ltd was a loan to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd.
In the table of 'capital' contributions, the $300,000 from Junk Yard Dog Ventures Pte Ltd was represented as funds of Teo Ying Ping.
When challenged on the representation of the $300,000 as funds of Teo Ying Ping, Ho Siow Poh stated that he was mistaken and had relied on 'accounts' that he could not identify.
The accounting records of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd reclassified the $300,000 contribution to reflect that it was a loan from Junk Yard Dog Ventures Pte Ltd.
On 14 January 2021, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd repaid a sum of $160,000 to or for the benefit of Teo Ying Ping.
The repayment of $160,000 on 14 January 2021 was not included in the table of 'capital' contributions prepared for AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd.
Under cross-examination, Ho Siow Poh sought to characterise the $160,000 payment on 14 January 2021 as a loan to Teo Ying Ping.

Teo Ying Ping testified that the $160,000 paid on 14 January 2021 did not belong to him and was due to others, and he accepted that AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd would not have paid him this sum if he had not requested it.
On 20 December 2022, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd repaid $15,000 to or for the benefit of Teo Ying Ping.
Teo Ying Ping was unable to explain why AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd paid him $15,000 on 20 December 2022.
On 8 October 2021, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd received a contribution of $260,000 associated with a hire-purchase loan taken by Teo Ying Ping over his car.
Under the hire-purchase loan taken by Teo Ying Ping, a total sum of $311,740, comprising principal and interest, was to be repaid across 60 monthly instalments.
The principal sum of $260,000 from the hire-purchase loan was paid to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, and approximately $60,000 was used for the renewal of the car’s Certificate of Entitlement.
AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd agreed to assume the monthly instalments of $5,196 under the hire-purchase loan on behalf of Teo Ying Ping and began making payments on 8 October 2021.
The monthly instalment payments by AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd under the hire-purchase loan ceased a few months after 8 October 2021 when the company ran out of funds.
Under cross-examination, Ho Siow Poh accepted that the hire-purchase transaction could be characterised as Teo Ying Ping lending approximately $200,000 to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd.
AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd entered into a loan agreement with Mengkim Holdings Pte Ltd for a loan of $500,000.

Ho Siow Poh testified that he, on behalf of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, entered into the loan agreement with Mengkim Holdings Pte Ltd because the company needed funds and it was in the company’s interests to take up the loan.

Teo Ying Ping was aware before signing that AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd was entering into the loan agreement with Mengkim Holdings Pte Ltd and did not object to the loan on the basis that the funds should have been provided personally by Liew Nyok Wah.

The internal accounting records and financial statements of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd recorded funds provided by Teo Ying Ping and Liew Nyok Wah as 'Loan from Director' or 'Loans from Directors'.
AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd pleaded that its accounting records were maintained by Liew Shulin and that the entries labelling contributions as loans were made to favour Liew Nyok Wah.
Teo Ying Ping deposed that in November 2020 he and Ho Siow Poh noticed that contributions were labelled as 'directors’ loans' in the records of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd.

Liew Shulin regularly prepared a 'loan sheet' shared in a WhatsApp chat group named 'AP Accounting Department', which included Teo Ying Ping and Marcus Teo, where monies received were recorded with headers such as 'Date of Loan', 'Expiry Date of Loan', and 'Loan interest/month'.
There is no evidence that Teo Ying Ping objected to the description of funds as loans in the loan sheets circulated in the 'AP Accounting Department' WhatsApp chat group.
After the departure of Liew Shulin from AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd in August 2023, Ho Siow Poh took over the financial documents and accounting records of the company.
On 9 January 2023, Ho Siow Poh signed a Creditors Confirmation Request confirming that AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd owed $1.03 million to Liew Nyok Wah as at 31 July 2022.

The most updated accounts of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd tendered in court, which the company intended to adopt at its Annual General Meeting, reflected amounts owed to Liew Nyok Wah and Teo Ying Ping as loans.
On 2 May 2023, a meeting took place between the three shareholders of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, and a recording and transcript of that meeting were produced in evidence.
In the 2 May 2023 meeting, there was a discussion between the shareholders about a possible buy-out between Teo Ying Ping and Liew Nyok Wah for a sum in the range of $500,000 to $700,000.

As of 8 October 2021, based on the company’s records, the net sums contributed by or through Teo Ying Ping and by or through Liew Nyok Wah, excluding initial capital injections, were approximately $2,124,407 and $1.8 million respectively.

The table of contributions in the affidavit of evidence-in-chief of Ho Siow Poh included an entry of an additional $300,000 contribution by Liew Nyok Wah on 2 August 2021 that was not supported by the company’s records or by the evidence of Liew Nyok Wah.

In its closing submissions, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd advanced a case that there was a 'Matching Dollar Agreement' under which the company and the shareholders agreed that the company would be funded on a dollar-to-dollar basis by contributions associated with Teo Ying Ping and Liew Nyok Wah.

In its closing submissions, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd asserted that under the Matching Dollar Agreement, funding could come from personal funds of the shareholders, loans taken by them and channelled to the company, or third-party loans obtained directly by the company.

In its closing submissions, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd stated that the shareholders did not truly think about or agree on whether the funding provided by Teo Ying Ping and Liew Nyok Wah was to be debt or equity.

As of 8 January 2021, the total funding associated with or provided by or through Liew Nyok Wah was approximately $1.3 million, while that associated with or provided by or through Teo Ying Ping was approximately $960,000.
On 8 January 2021, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd made a withdrawal of $160,000 associated with or for the benefit of Teo Ying Ping, thereby reducing his net contribution.

In 2022, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd made repayments of various third-party loans and shareholder-related loans without any recorded adjustment or rationalisation by reference to a matching contribution arrangement.
The loans from Mengkim Holdings Pte Ltd and Junk Yard Dog Ventures Pte Ltd were reclassified in the accounting records of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd as external loans.
Loans associated with or funded by third parties Tan Bee Hong and Ban Leong remained classified in the accounting records of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd under 'Loan from Director'.
The repayment date of the loan from Mengkim Holdings Pte Ltd to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd was extended first to 8 April 2022 and then to 8 July 2022.
The loan from Mengkim Holdings Pte Ltd was repaid by AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd in instalments, with the final repayment made on 28 April 2023.
Between 21 November 2021 and 21 January 2022, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd repaid $200,000 to or in respect of a loan from Lee Sze Peng, a friend of Ho Siow Poh.
Between 19 November 2021 and May 2022, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd repaid $200,000 to WQ Leasing Pte Ltd.
Between 1 June 2022 and 12 July 2022, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd repaid $200,000 to Junk Yard Dog Ventures Pte Ltd in two parts.
On 31 May 2022, WhatsApp messages between Marcus Teo and Liew Shulin showed that the repayment to Junk Yard Dog Ventures Pte Ltd was structured in two parts to accommodate pending car sales.
AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd operated a fleet of cars which were sold over time, including sales commencing in or around November 2020.
According to the evidence of Ho Siow Poh, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd raised just over $1 million from the sale of its fleet of cars after paying off loans taken to purchase the cars.
AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd paid interest at rates up to 18% per annum on certain loans, including loans associated with or provided through Teo Ying Ping.

AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd pleaded a claim for losses due to the difference between the interest rate on third-party loans associated with Liew Nyok Wah and an 8% per annum rate allegedly applicable to shareholder funding.
AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd pleaded that it suffered loss due to an alleged 'fire sale' of approximately 100 cars from its fleet in order to pay off a loan in the name of Mengkim Holdings Pte Ltd.

AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd pleaded that it suffered loss due to the loss of use of $900,000 repaid to third parties plus $331,500 in interest payments, and quantified alleged damages by reference to the difference in net profit after tax between two financial years.

AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd pleaded that it suffered loss due to the payment of monthly retainer fees to Jackspeed Automobile (S) Pte Ltd for the period March 2021 to May 2023, which it alleged were wrongfully charged.
AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd pleaded a claim for $300,000 in respect of a loan made by the company to one Chris Eng that had not been repaid.
AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd moved its workshop operations from premises in Tampines to premises in Loyang in early 2021.

After moving its workshop operations to Loyang, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd retained some space at the Tampines premises, including an office unit on the second storey and a room on the first storey.
The accounting and other records of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd continued to be stored at the Tampines premises after the move to Loyang.
The Tampines premises remained the registered office of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd until 24 August 2023.
AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd paid a monthly retainer fee to Jackspeed Automobile (S) Pte Ltd for, among other things, the rental of office space at the Tampines premises.
Teo Ying Ping testified that the three shareholders discussed moving operations to Loyang and that there was an agreement to retain financial operations at the Tampines premises.

Under cross-examination, both Teo Ying Ping and Ho Siow Poh confirmed that AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd agreed to continue paying a reduced retainer fee to Jackspeed Automobile (S) Pte Ltd after moving to the Loyang premises.
AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd pleaded that it had paid interest of $112,000 to Bizmen Corporation Ltd in relation to a loan.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the funding provided to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd by or through its shareholders, particularly the contributions associated with Liew Nyok Wah and Teo Ying Ping, constituted capital injections subject to a binding matching contribution agreement or constituted loans, and whether any such agreement or funding structure imposed enforceable obligations on the shareholders that could ground a claim by the company for repayment-related losses or other damages.
Party_Details.Rule: In company law, the characterization of shareholder funding as equity or debt depends on the parties’ objective intentions as evidenced by contemporaneous documents, accounting treatment, agreed terms on repayment and interest, and the conduct of the parties, rather than on ex post labels used in litigation. A company may only sue on an agreement to which it is a party and has standing to enforce. An alleged shareholders’ agreement or funding arrangement must be properly pleaded and proved, and any implied term must satisfy the usual tests for implication in fact, including necessity for business efficacy and obviousness, and must be pleaded. Where funds are advanced as loans, the lender is generally entitled to repayment in accordance with the agreed terms, and the mere fact that the lender is a shareholder does not, without more, restrict repayment. Claims for damages must be supported by a coherent legal basis and evidence of causation and quantification, and cannot be premised on speculative or arbitrary calculations.
Party_Details.Application: The contemporaneous accounting records and financial statements of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd consistently recorded the contributions associated with or provided by or through the shareholders as 'Loan from Director' or 'Loans from Directors', and the shareholders, including the Managing Director, did not cause these records to be amended even after noticing the descriptions. The company’s filings with the Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority showed that its share capital remained at $100,000, and there was no documentary record of any increase in paid-up capital beyond the initial capital injections. Specific transactions, such as the $100,000 advanced by cheque on 7 April 2021 with an agreed short-term repayment and interest of 1.5% per month, the $300,000 loan from Junk Yard Dog Ventures Pte Ltd, and the hire-purchase arrangement over the car of Teo Ying Ping, were structured and treated as loans with agreed interest and repayment schedules, and were in some instances partially repaid. The company’s own internal loan sheets, circulated in the 'AP Accounting Department' WhatsApp group, described the funding as loans with dates, expiry dates, and monthly interest, and no objections were raised by the shareholders at the time. The pleaded Agreement that the shareholders would make matching capital injections was not supported by any written document, and the evidence showed that contributions were made in an ad hoc manner, with significant differences in net contributions at various points and with withdrawals by one shareholder without any contemporaneous demand for matching by the other. The later 'Matching Dollar Agreement' theory advanced in closing submissions was not pleaded and was defined by reference to what the parties had done rather than to any proven consensus on binding obligations, and the company itself acknowledged that the shareholders had not clearly agreed whether the funding was debt or equity. The company’s own conduct in repaying shareholder-related loans and third-party loans, including repayments to or for the benefit of Teo Ying Ping and repayments to external lenders such as Junk Yard Dog Ventures Pte Ltd, WQ Leasing Pte Ltd, and Lee Sze Peng, without any recorded insistence on replacement funding or matching contributions, was consistent with the funding being loans repayable in the ordinary course. On these facts, the general principles on characterization of funding and enforceability of agreements indicate that the contributions associated with or provided by or through the shareholders, beyond the initial paid-up capital, were loans rather than capital injections, and that AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd did not establish a binding matching capital or funding agreement or any implied restriction on repayment that could support its pleaded heads of loss.
Party_Details.Conclusion: The funding provided to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd by or through its shareholders beyond the initial paid-up capital was properly characterized as loans rather than capital injections, no binding matching capital or funding agreement or implied restriction on repayment was established on the evidence and pleadings, and the company therefore lacked a sustainable basis to claim the pleaded repayment-related losses and other damages from its shareholder-lender.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court examined the pleaded Agreement and noted that AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd’s own case was that the agreement to fund the company was between the three shareholders and not with the company, which raised a standing issue for the company’s claim. The judge found that there was no written evidence of the alleged Agreement and that the oral evidence in support of it was weak and inconsistent. In particular, the judge considered that while Ho Siow Poh’s affidavit suggested a commitment by the shareholders to inject $500,000 each as capital, his cross-examination revealed that only an initial $50,000 each had been agreed as capital, undermining the pleaded case of a larger capital commitment. The judge also regarded the use of the phrase 'funds or capital injections' in Ho’s affidavit as ambiguous and inconsistent with a clear equity-only arrangement. The court placed significant weight on the company’s financial statements and ACRA filings, which showed that the paid-up capital remained at $100,000, and found the explanation that this was due to Ho’s focus on operations unconvincing, especially given his own evidence that counterparties would look to paid-up capital as a measure of viability. The judge considered the agreed payment of interest on shareholder contributions, and the fact that interest was only paid after the initial $100,000 capital injection, as strongly indicative that subsequent contributions were loans rather than equity. The court analysed specific transactions, including the $100,000 advanced by cheque on 7 April 2021, which was expressly described in a WhatsApp message as a short-term loan with a defined interest rate and repayment period, and the $300,000 from Junk Yard Dog Ventures Pte Ltd, which was conceded by Teo to be a loan to the company and was reclassified in the company’s accounts as such. The judge found that the representation of the $300,000 as a capital contribution by Teo in the table of contributions and in the affidavits was inaccurate and that Ho’s explanation of reliance on unidentified 'accounts' was unsatisfactory. The court also noted that part of the funds associated with Teo were repaid, including the $160,000 on 14 January 2021 and $15,000 on 20 December 2022, and that these repayments were inconsistent with the company’s and Teo’s characterisation of the contributions as capital, since capital is not ordinarily repayable in that manner. The hire-purchase arrangement over Teo’s car was analysed in detail, and the judge concluded that the structure, under which the company assumed the obligation to pay instalments comprising principal and interest, effectively meant that the company was repaying Teo’s contribution, which was more consistent with a loan than with a capital injection. The judge further observed that the company and the shareholders did not object when Liew procured third-party loans, such as the Mengkim loan, to meet funding needs, and that Teo himself arranged for the company to borrow from Junk Yard Dog Ventures Pte Ltd, which undermined the notion of a strict obligation on Liew to inject personal capital. The court rejected the explanation that the accounting records labelling contributions as 'Loan from Director' were manipulated by Liew through his daughter, noting that these entries predated the dispute, that Ho and Teo had access to and knowledge of the records, that they did not instruct any amendments, and that updated accounts prepared for adoption at the Annual General Meeting continued to reflect the amounts as loans. The judge also considered the regular 'loan sheets' prepared by Liew Shulin and shared with Teo and Marcus Teo, which described the funding as loans with specific interest terms, and found the absence of any contemporaneous objection telling. The court reviewed the transcript of the 2 May 2023 meeting and concluded that, even if Liew had acknowledged some form of matching expectation, the transcript did not clearly address the legal nature of the contributions, and there was no contemporaneous allegation in that discussion that Liew had wrongfully procured repayments. The judge accepted that there may have been an expectation or understanding that Teo and Liew would fund the company to the best of their ability, but held that this did not amount to a legally enforceable obligation. The court rejected the 'Matching Dollar Agreement' theory advanced for the first time in closing submissions as unpleaded and inconsistent with the evidence, noting that contributions were not in fact kept matched, that there was no attempt to enforce matching when one shareholder was ahead or behind, and that repayments in 2022 were not rationalised by reference to any matching arrangement. The judge also rejected the attempt to imply a term restricting withdrawals until the company turned a profit or remained sufficiently funded, distinguishing the case of Wee Kah Lee on the basis that the loans there were for a specific development project with a defined time frame and that the implied term had been pleaded. In contrast, the funding in this case was for general operations, and the company’s own conduct in repaying shareholder-related loans and third-party loans, including short-term loans and the hire-purchase instalments, was inconsistent with any such implied restriction. The court found that the heads of loss pleaded by the company, including losses due to interest rate differences, alleged fire-sale losses, loss of use of funds, and retainer fees, lacked a coherent legal or evidential foundation, and in some instances were based on incorrect factual premises or arbitrary calculations. Overall, applying the general principles on characterization of funding, standing, pleading requirements, and proof of damages, the judge concluded that the contributions beyond the initial paid-up capital were loans, that no binding matching funding agreement or implied restriction on repayment was established, and that AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd’s claims could not succeed on the facts and law.

Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Liew Nyok Wah

Facts.Text: Liew Nyok Wah is the defendant in Originating Claim No 475 of 2023 brought by AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd.
Liew Nyok Wah is the claimant in the counterclaim against AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd.
At the time of incorporation of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, Liew Nyok Wah held 33% of the shares in the company.
Liew Shulin Mrs Alexander Chong is the daughter of Liew Nyok Wah.
Shortly after the incorporation of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, the daughter of Liew Nyok Wah joined the company and was involved in its finance and business operations.
The employment of the daughter of Liew Nyok Wah with AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd was terminated on 27 July 2023.
Throughout the life of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, funds were contributed to the company by or through Liew Nyok Wah.
The initial capital injection by or associated with Liew Nyok Wah at the incorporation of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd was $50,000.
Beyond the initial capital injection, further funds associated with or provided by or through Liew Nyok Wah were advanced to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd.

As of 8 October 2021, the net sum contributed by or through Liew Nyok Wah to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, excluding the initial capital injection, was approximately $1.8 million according to the company’s records.

AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd entered into a loan agreement with Mengkim Holdings Pte Ltd for a loan of $500,000, which the company associated with the funding obligations of or through Liew Nyok Wah.

Ho Siow Poh testified that he agreed to the Mengkim Holdings Pte Ltd loan on behalf of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd because the company needed funds and it was in the company’s interests to take up the loan.

The internal accounting records and financial statements of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd recorded funds associated with or provided by or through Liew Nyok Wah as 'Loan from Director' or 'Loans from Directors'.
On 9 January 2023, a Creditors Confirmation Request signed by Ho Siow Poh confirmed that AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd owed $1.03 million to Liew Nyok Wah as at 31 July 2022.
The most updated accounts of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd tendered in court reflected amounts owed to Liew Nyok Wah as loans.
On 2 May 2023, a meeting took place between the shareholders of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, including Liew Nyok Wah, and a recording and transcript of that meeting were produced in evidence.
During the 2 May 2023 meeting, there was a discussion between Liew Nyok Wah and Teo Ying Ping about a possible buy-out of one by the other for a sum between $500,000 and $700,000.
In his affidavit of evidence-in-chief, Liew Nyok Wah stated that only the initial $100,000 contributed by the shareholders was a capital injection and that his subsequent contributions were loans.

In his affidavit of evidence-in-chief, Liew Nyok Wah stated that the term he used in Mandarin during the 2 May 2023 meeting, which was translated as 'capital' in the transcript, could also mean 'principal sum'.
After the falling out among the shareholders in or around May 2023, there is no evidence that Liew Nyok Wah was involved in the management of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd.
On 25 July 2023, AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd commenced legal proceedings against Liew Nyok Wah in the General Division of the High Court of Singapore.
At trial, evidence in support of the defence and counterclaim of Liew Nyok Wah was given by himself and by his daughter.
The accounting records of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd included a 'Loan from Director' ledger in which loans associated with or provided by or through Liew Nyok Wah were recorded.

The loans from third parties Tan Bee Hong and Ban Leong, which were associated with funding arranged by or through Liew Nyok Wah, were recorded in the accounting records of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd under 'Loan from Director'.

AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd made repayments in respect of loans associated with or provided by or through Liew Nyok Wah, including repayments of loans from third parties such as Tan Bee Hong and Ban Leong.
Ho Siow Poh testified that he was aware of and approved the repayments made by AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd in respect of loans associated with or provided by or through Liew Nyok Wah.
In his counterclaim, Liew Nyok Wah claimed repayment of loans advanced by or through him to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd in the sum of $719,000.
AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd did not dispute the quantum of $719,000 claimed by Liew Nyok Wah as the outstanding amount of his loans in the counterclaim.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the advances and funding associated with or provided by or through Liew Nyok Wah to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, beyond his initial capital injection, were loans entitling him to repayment from the company, and whether any alleged funding agreement or fiduciary obligation could lawfully restrict or negate his right to repayment of those loans.
Party_Details.Rule: Where a shareholder or related party advances funds to a company, the legal characterization of those advances as loans or capital contributions is determined by the objective evidence, including the terms of the advance, the presence of interest and repayment obligations, the accounting treatment, and the parties’ conduct. If the advances are loans, the lender is generally entitled to repayment in accordance with the agreed terms, and the company cannot deny repayment absent a valid contractual or statutory restriction. Any alleged agreement that modifies or restricts repayment rights must be properly pleaded, must be shown to bind the company, and must be proved by cogent evidence. A company that has treated advances as loans in its accounts, confirmed the indebtedness in creditor confirmations, and made repayments in the ordinary course will ordinarily be held to that characterization. A counterclaim for repayment of loans will succeed where the existence and quantum of the loans are admitted or proved and no valid defence is established.
Party_Details.Application: The internal accounting records and financial statements of AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd consistently recorded the funds associated with or provided by or through Liew Nyok Wah as 'Loan from Director' or 'Loans from Directors', and these records were maintained over time without amendment by the company’s management, including after the departure of his daughter from the finance function. On 9 January 2023, the Managing Director, Ho Siow Poh, signed a Creditors Confirmation Request expressly confirming that the company owed $1.03 million to Liew Nyok Wah as at 31 July 2022, which is a direct acknowledgment of indebtedness. The most recent accounts tendered in court, which the company intended to adopt at its Annual General Meeting, continued to show amounts owed to Liew Nyok Wah as loans, and there was no evidence that the company reclassified these amounts as equity. The company’s own conduct included making repayments in respect of loans associated with or provided by or through Liew Nyok Wah, including repayments of third-party loans such as those from Tan Bee Hong and Ban Leong, with the knowledge and approval of the Managing Director. In his affidavit of evidence-in-chief, Liew Nyok Wah stated that only the initial $100,000 contributed by the shareholders was capital and that his subsequent contributions were loans, and this position was consistent with the accounting treatment and the payment of interest on those contributions. The company did not dispute in the pleadings or at trial that the outstanding sum of $719,000 claimed by Liew Nyok Wah in his counterclaim represented loans advanced by or through him to the company. Although AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd pleaded and later advanced theories of an Agreement or a Matching Dollar Agreement that would allegedly affect the treatment of funding, those theories were not supported by contemporaneous documentation, were inconsistent with the company’s own records and conduct, and were not shown to create a binding restriction on the repayment of loans advanced by or through Liew Nyok Wah. Applying the general principles on characterization of advances and enforceability of agreements, the objective evidence supports the conclusion that the advances beyond the initial capital injection were loans, that AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd acknowledged its indebtedness to Liew Nyok Wah, and that no valid contractual or statutory defence was established to his claim for repayment of $719,000.
Party_Details.Conclusion: The advances and funding associated with or provided by or through Liew Nyok Wah to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd beyond his initial capital injection were loans, and in the absence of any proven binding restriction or defence, he was entitled to repayment of the outstanding sum of $719,000 from the company.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court began by considering the nature of the funding associated with or provided by or through Liew Nyok Wah to AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd, in light of the company’s broader argument that such funding formed part of a matching capital or funding arrangement. The judge noted that the company’s own accounting records and financial statements consistently described the contributions associated with or provided by or through Liew as 'Loan from Director' or 'Loans from Directors', and that these records were prepared and maintained over time, including during the period when the relationship among the shareholders was amicable. The court rejected the company’s suggestion that these entries were manipulated by Liew through his daughter, pointing out that the entries predated the dispute, that the Managing Director and the other shareholder had access to and knowledge of the records, and that they did not instruct any amendments even after allegedly noticing the descriptions. The judge attached particular significance to the Creditors Confirmation Request signed by Ho Siow Poh on 9 January 2023, which confirmed that the company owed $1.03 million to Liew as at 31 July 2022, treating it as a clear acknowledgment of indebtedness. The court also observed that the most recent accounts tendered in court, which the company intended to adopt at its Annual General Meeting, continued to reflect the amounts owed to Liew as loans, and that no reasonable explanation was given for the failure to reclassify them as equity if the company genuinely believed they were capital contributions. The judge considered the evidence that the company had made repayments in respect of loans associated with or provided by or through Liew, including repayments of third-party loans such as those from Tan Bee Hong and Ban Leong, and found that these repayments were authorised by the Managing Director. The court found it difficult to reconcile the company’s decision to sue Liew over repayments that had been approved by its own Managing Director with any coherent theory of breach of duty by Liew. The judge noted that the company’s counsel expressly disavowed any case based on duress, undue influence, or similar vitiating factors in relation to the Managing Director’s decisions to approve the repayments. The court examined the broader context of the alleged Agreement and Matching Dollar Agreement and concluded that these theories did not alter the legal characterization of Liew’s advances as loans. The judge found that the alleged matching arrangements were either unpleaded or unsupported by contemporaneous evidence, and that the company’s own conduct in treating the advances as loans, paying interest, and making repayments was inconsistent with any binding restriction on repayment. The court also took into account Liew’s evidence that only the initial $100,000 was capital and that his subsequent contributions were loans, and found that this evidence was corroborated by the accounting treatment and by the pattern of interest payments. The judge noted that AP Automotive Services Pte Ltd did not dispute the quantum of $719,000 claimed by Liew in his counterclaim as representing the outstanding amount of his loans. Applying the general principles that advances objectively structured and treated as loans are repayable absent a valid restriction, and that a company is bound by its own acknowledgments of indebtedness, the court concluded that the advances beyond the initial capital injection were loans and that the company had no defence to Liew’s counterclaim for repayment of $719,000.
